network.overall_strategy: A comprehensive prebiotic synthesis network for glycine (NH₂CH₂COOH) built from CO₂ and H₂ as ultimate feedstocks. The network features two parallel hydrothermal CO₂-fixation branches (greigite-catalyzed formate production and magnetite-catalyzed Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis) that converge on formaldehyde, the primary C1 hub. Formaldehyde then feeds four distinct surface-chemistry modules: (1) direct Strecker synthesis to aminoacetonitrile, (2) cyanosulfidic glycolonitrile formation followed by ammonolysis to aminoacetonitrile, (3) mechanistic Strecker via aminomethanol to aminoacetonitrile, and (4) formose-to-glycolaldehyde oxidation chain to glyoxylate. The aminoacetonitrile branch and glyoxylate branch each terminate in a distinct biochemical reaction producing glycine—aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis and glyoxylate reductive amination, respectively—giving two independent final routes to the target. All environmental flow is strictly Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical with no regressions. Every hydrothermal reaction specifies iron-sulfur or iron-oxide mineral catalysts, and every surface reaction specifies clay, borate, sulfide, or TiO₂ mineral agents, as dictated by the literature.

reactions.name: CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, pH 9-11, H₂-rich fluids from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is adsorbed onto greigite surface where it is reduced by H₂ via a formate intermediate. The [Fe₄S₄]-like cluster structure of greigite provides the electron transfer pathway analogous to modern carbon monoxide dehydrogenase/acetyl-CoA synthase. The mineral surface stabilizes the transition state for C–H bond formation.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO₂ reduction to formate is well-established as an analog of biological CO₂ fixation. Preiner et al. (2020) demonstrated CO₂ reduction to formate on native iron and iron sulfide surfaces under hydrothermal conditions. The structural similarity between greigite and biological [Fe₄S₄] clusters supports this pathway (Russell & Martin, 2004; Roldan et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Reverse water-gas shift reaction on magnetite

conditions.other: Black smoker vent conditions, high-temperature iron oxide surfaces

reactions.mechanism: The reverse water-gas shift reaction proceeds on magnetite surfaces at elevated temperature. CO₂ adsorbs on Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ sites and is reduced by H₂ to yield CO and H₂O. Magnetite provides mixed-valence iron sites that facilitate electron transfer.
reactions.reasoning: The reverse water-gas shift reaction is thermodynamically favorable at high temperatures on magnetite surfaces. This reaction is a prerequisite for Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis and has been demonstrated under simulated hydrothermal conditions (McCollom & Seewald, 2006; He et al., 2019). Magnetite's mixed-valence iron sites (Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺) make it an effective redox catalyst.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of formaldehyde on magnetite

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type conditions at black smoker interfaces; formaldehyde is a C1 oxygenate product

reactions.mechanism: CO adsorbs on magnetite surface, undergoes partial hydrogenation via dissociatively adsorbed H₂, yielding formaldehyde (HCHO) as the simplest oxygenated product. The reaction proceeds through a surface-bound formyl (HCO*) intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis on magnetite produces a range of oxygenated C1 products including formaldehyde, methanol, and formic acid. FTT synthesis has been demonstrated under hydrothermal conditions by McCollom et al. (1999) and Rushdi & Simoneit (2001). Formaldehyde is a critical C1 building block for subsequent prebiotic chemistry.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on native iron

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal conditions; native iron and FeS provide strong reducing environment; H₂ serves as co-reductant

reactions.mechanism: Formate is adsorbed on the surface of native iron (Fe⁰) and reduced by a combination of surface-donated electrons and dissolved H₂. The carboxylate oxygen is reduced, yielding formaldehyde. Mackinawite (FeS) co-catalyzes the reaction by facilitating electron transfer. This is thermodynamically challenging but driven by the strong reducing power of Fe⁰ and high H₂ fugacity.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) showed that native iron and iron sulfide minerals can reduce CO₂ beyond formate to formaldehyde and methanol under hydrothermal conditions when H₂ fugacity is high. The strong reducing potential of Fe⁰ (E° = -0.44 V) combined with alkaline vent pH gradients drives the thermodynamically uphill reduction of formate to formaldehyde. Mackinawite serves as a co-catalyst facilitating multi-electron reduction (Herschy et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of aminoacetonitrile from formaldehyde

conditions.other: Alkaline to neutral pH (pH 7-9); aqueous solution in evaporitic pool; montmorillonite concentrates reactants on interlayer surfaces

reactions.mechanism: The classic Strecker synthesis proceeds in three steps on the clay surface: (1) Formaldehyde condenses with ammonia to form an imine (methyleneimine, H₂C=NH), releasing water. (2) HCN adds across the C=N bond via nucleophilic addition, forming aminoacetonitrile (NH₂CH₂CN). The carbon atom from formaldehyde becomes the α-carbon, and the carbon from HCN becomes the carboxyl-precursor carbon. Montmorillonite catalyzes the condensation by concentrating reactants and stabilizing the imine intermediate on its negatively charged interlayer surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is the most well-established prebiotic route to amino acids (Miller, 1957; Strecker, 1850). Formaldehyde reacts with NH₃ and HCN to form aminoacetonitrile as a key intermediate, which is subsequently hydrolyzed to glycine. The reaction proceeds at mild temperatures (300-350K) in aqueous conditions at neutral to alkaline pH. Montmorillonite clay catalyzes condensation reactions by concentrating organics on mineral surfaces (Ferris et al., 1996; Lambert, 2008). Isotopic labeling confirms that formaldehyde provides the α-carbon and HCN provides the carboxyl carbon of glycine.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic glycolonitrile formation from formaldehyde and HCN

conditions.other: Cyanosulfidic conditions; slightly acidic to neutral pH; stibnite provides sulfide surface for HCN activation

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde reacts with HCN via nucleophilic cyanohydrin addition. The cyanide ion (CN⁻) attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of formaldehyde, forming 2-hydroxyacetonitrile (glycolonitrile, HOCH₂CN). The sulfide mineral surface activates HCN by promoting its deprotonation to CN⁻. This is the simplest cyanohydrin reaction and the first step in the cyanosulfidic protometabolism network.
reactions.reasoning: Glycolonitrile formation from formaldehyde and HCN is a key step in cyanosulfidic protometabolism as described by Patel et al. (2015) and Sutherland (2017). The cyanohydrin formation is thermodynamically favorable in aqueous solution. Sulfide minerals like stibnite facilitate HCN chemistry in the Powner-Sutherland cyanosulfidic network. Glycolonitrile is identified as a critical intermediate that can be polymerized or transformed into amino acid precursors.

reactions.name: Ammonolysis of glycolonitrile to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: Alkaline pH (8-10) favors NH₃ nucleophilicity; clay surface concentrates reactants and stabilizes transition state

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia displaces the hydroxyl group of glycolonitrile (HOCH₂CN) via nucleophilic substitution at the α-carbon, forming aminoacetonitrile (NH₂CH₂CN) and water. Under alkaline conditions, NH₃ is a competent nucleophile and the hydroxyl is a reasonable leaving group when activated by the electron-withdrawing nitrile group. This reaction bridges the cyanosulfidic and Strecker pathways by converting a cyanohydrin to an aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The interconversion of cyanohydrins and aminonitriles is documented in prebiotic chemistry. Under alkaline conditions with ammonia, glycolonitrile can be converted to aminoacetonitrile, which is the key Strecker intermediate. This represents the intersection of the cyanosulfidic (Patel et al., 2015) and Strecker (Miller, 1957) pathways. Montmorillonite catalyzes the substitution by concentrating reactants on its interlayer surfaces (Lambert, 2008). The equilibrium between cyanohydrin and aminonitrile depends on pH and ammonia concentration.

reactions.name: Formose condensation of formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: Alkaline conditions (pH 10-12) with Ca(OH)₂; borate minerals stabilize products and prevent over-polymerization; warm evaporitic pool environment

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of formaldehyde undergo an aldol condensation to form glycolaldehyde (HOCH₂CHO). The base Ca(OH)₂ deprotonates one formaldehyde at the α-position, generating a carbanion (or enolate equivalent) that attacks the carbonyl of a second formaldehyde. Borate minerals complex the glycolaldehyde product via 1,2-diol complexation, stabilizing it against further polymerization and channeling the formose reaction toward C2 products.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction is a well-documented prebiotic pathway for sugar synthesis from formaldehyde (Butlerow, 1861). The key challenge is selectivity—without borate minerals, the formose reaction produces a complex tar. Ricardo et al. (2004) demonstrated that borate minerals stabilize specific sugars (including glycolaldehyde and ribose) by forming borate ester complexes. Ca(OH)₂ is the classical formose catalyst. This reaction has been shown to proceed at 300-370K under alkaline conditions (Kopetzki & Antonietti, 2011).

reactions.name: UV-photooxidation of glycolaldehyde to glycolic acid

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 300 nm, no ozone shield on early Earth); shallow tidal pool with TiO₂-bearing sediments; anoxic conditions—oxidant is photogenerated OH· radical from water splitting on TiO₂

reactions.mechanism: UV radiation excites TiO₂ (anatase), generating electron-hole pairs. The photogenerated holes oxidize adsorbed water to produce hydroxyl radicals (OH·). These radicals oxidize the aldehyde group of glycolaldehyde (HOCH₂CHO) to a carboxylic acid, yielding glycolic acid (HOCH₂COOH). The TiO₂ surface provides both the photocatalytic oxidant generation and a binding surface for the substrate.
reactions.reasoning: TiO₂ is a well-known UV photocatalyst that can drive oxidation reactions in aqueous solution via hydroxyl radical generation. Anatase TiO₂ is a common mineral on early Earth surfaces. UV-driven photooxidation of aldehydes to carboxylic acids is a standard photocatalytic process. On the early Earth, the lack of an ozone layer meant intense UV radiation (λ < 300 nm) reaching the surface, making TiO₂ photocatalysis highly relevant (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012). The oxidation of glycolaldehyde to glycolic acid is also documented in hydrothermal formaldehyde-ammonia experiments (Kebukawa et al., 2017).

reactions.name: UV-photooxidation of glycolic acid to glyoxylate

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 300 nm); shallow tidal pool; TiO₂ photocatalysis generates OH· radicals that oxidize the α-hydroxyl to an aldehyde/carboxylate; anoxic conditions

reactions.mechanism: UV-excited TiO₂ generates hydroxyl radicals that selectively oxidize the α-CH₂OH group of glycolic acid (HOCH₂COOH) to an aldehyde, yielding glyoxylic acid (OHCCOOH), which dissociates to glyoxylate (OHCCOO⁻) at near-neutral to alkaline pH. The selectivity arises because the α-hydroxyl C–H bonds are more labile to radical abstraction than the carboxyl group.
reactions.reasoning: Photocatalytic oxidation of glycolic acid to glyoxylic acid is documented in the photocatalysis literature and is prebiotically plausible given TiO₂ surfaces and intense UV flux on early Earth. Glyoxylate is a central intermediate in prebiotic protometabolism, serving as a precursor to glycine via reductive amination (Springsteen et al., 2018; Stubbs et al., 2020). The two-step oxidation from glycolaldehyde through glycolic acid to glyoxylate provides a controlled route to this key α-keto acid.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glycolonitrile to glycolic acid

conditions.other: Warm evaporitic pool conditions; mildly alkaline pH (8-10) promotes nitrile hydrolysis; clay mineral surface catalyzes hydrolysis by Lewis acid activation of the nitrile

reactions.mechanism: Glycolonitrile (HOCH₂CN) undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: (1) Water attacks the nitrile carbon, forming a glycolamide intermediate (HOCH₂CONH₂). (2) Further hydrolysis of the amide yields glycolic acid (HOCH₂COOH) and ammonia (NH₃). The clay mineral surface acts as a Lewis acid catalyst, activating the nitrile group toward nucleophilic addition of water. Note that the released NH₃ can be recycled into other nitrogen-requiring reactions in the network.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis is a standard aqueous reaction that proceeds at elevated temperature and alkaline pH. In prebiotic chemistry, the hydrolysis of cyanohydrins to hydroxy acids is well-documented as an alternative to the aminonitrile → amino acid pathway (Eschenmoser, 2007). Glycolonitrile hydrolysis yields glycolic acid, a key intermediate identified in the literature as a glycine precursor through the glycolic acid → glyoxylate route (Kebukawa et al., 2017). Montmorillonite catalyzes nitrile hydrolysis via Lewis acid activation (Lambert, 2008).

reactions.name: Condensation of formaldehyde and ammonia to aminomethanol

conditions.other: Neutral to slightly alkaline pH; aqueous solution in evaporitic pool; clay concentrates reactants; this is the first mechanistic step of the Strecker synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia acts as a nucleophile, attacking the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of formaldehyde. The resulting addition product is aminomethanol (NH₂CH₂OH), a hemiaminal. Under aqueous conditions at moderate pH, aminomethanol is in equilibrium with the dehydration product methyleneimine (H₂C=NH), but the hemiaminal is the dominant species in dilute aqueous solution.
reactions.reasoning: Aminomethanol (NH₂CH₂OH) has been experimentally identified as a key intermediate in the Strecker synthesis of glycine (Danger et al., 2011). The condensation of formaldehyde with ammonia to form aminomethanol or the corresponding imine is the first step of the Strecker mechanism. Under prebiotic aqueous conditions, this equilibrium is established rapidly. Montmorillonite clay surfaces concentrate the reactants and can shift the equilibrium toward the condensation product (Lambert, 2008).

reactions.name: HCN addition to aminomethanol forming aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: Neutral to alkaline pH promotes CN⁻ nucleophilicity; aminomethanol → imine → aminoacetonitrile; clay surface template-directs the addition

reactions.mechanism: Aminomethanol (NH₂CH₂OH) partially dehydrates to methyleneimine (H₂C=NH) on the clay surface. Cyanide ion (CN⁻, from HCN deprotonation at alkaline pH) attacks the electrophilic carbon of the imine, forming aminoacetonitrile (NH₂CH₂CN). This is the second key step of the mechanistic Strecker synthesis. The overall transformation is: NH₂CH₂OH + HCN → NH₂CH₂CN + H₂O.
reactions.reasoning: This mechanistic variant of the Strecker synthesis separates the overall HCHO + NH₃ + HCN reaction into its individual steps, as documented by Danger et al. (2011) who experimentally identified aminomethanol as a discrete intermediate. The two-step Strecker mechanism (hemiaminal formation → cyanide addition) is more mechanistically realistic than a single-step three-component reaction. Montmorillonite catalyzes both the dehydration and the cyanide addition (Lambert, 2008).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine

conditions.other: Prebiotic pool environment; neutral to alkaline pH; thermal cycling promotes hydrolysis; released NH₃ is recycled in the network; proceeds through glycinamide (NH₂CH₂CONH₂) as intermediate

reactions.mechanism: Aminoacetonitrile (NH₂CH₂CN) undergoes stepwise hydrolysis in aqueous solution: (1) Water attacks the nitrile carbon, forming glycinamide (NH₂CH₂CONH₂) as an intermediate. (2) Further hydrolysis of the amide yields glycine (NH₂CH₂COOH) and ammonia (NH₃). The overall reaction: NH₂CH₂CN + 2H₂O → NH₂CH₂COOH + NH₃. The released ammonia can re-enter the synthesis network. Thermal cycling in a prebiotic pool (geothermal environment) accelerates the hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis to glycine is the terminal step of the Strecker synthesis and is well-established in prebiotic chemistry (Miller, 1957; Strecker, 1850). The hydrolysis proceeds through a glycinamide intermediate and is accelerated at elevated temperature and alkaline pH. Energy barriers to nitrile hydrolysis have been noted in the literature, but thermal cycling in geothermal pools provides sufficient activation energy (Danger et al., 2011). This reaction was detected in Miller's original spark discharge experiments and in meteorite parent body aqueous alteration models.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.other: Prebiotic pool; pH 7-8; Fe⁰ (metallic iron) as stoichiometric reductant provides the two electrons needed for reductive amination; temperature ~50°C (323K) as per experimental conditions; geothermal pool with iron-rich sediments

reactions.mechanism: Glyoxylate (OHCCOO⁻) reacts with ammonia (NH₃) to form an imine intermediate (HNCOO⁻ → glycine imine). Metallic iron (Fe⁰) acts as a stoichiometric two-electron reductant, donating electrons to reduce the C=N bond of the imine, yielding glycine (NH₂CH₂COO⁻). The overall reaction: OHCCOO⁻ + NH₃ + 2[H] (from Fe⁰) → NH₂CH₂COO⁻ + H₂O. This is a direct analog of biological transaminase/aminotransferase chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of glyoxylate to glycine has been experimentally demonstrated by Barge et al. (2019) using metallic iron (Fe⁰) as a reductant and hydroxylamine as a nitrogen source, and by Springsteen et al. (2018) who showed transamination of glyoxylate with other amino acids at pH 8 and 50°C. The reaction proceeds via an imine intermediate and is a direct prebiotic analog of biological transamination. Glyoxylate is identified as a key α-keto acid hub in protometabolic networks (Stubbs et al., 2020). The reductive amination route provides an independent pathway to glycine that does not require HCN.

pathways.name: Formate–Strecker pathway (direct)
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced to formate on greigite in hydrothermal vents, formate is further reduced to formaldehyde on native iron, and formaldehyde undergoes direct Strecker synthesis with HCN and NH₃ on montmorillonite clay in surface environments, producing aminoacetonitrile which is hydrolyzed to glycine in prebiotic pools.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch–Strecker pathway
pathways.description: CO₂ is converted to CO via reverse water-gas shift on magnetite, CO is reduced to formaldehyde via Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite, and formaldehyde undergoes direct Strecker synthesis with HCN and NH₃ on clay, producing aminoacetonitrile which is hydrolyzed to glycine.

pathways.name: Formate–cyanosulfidic–ammonolysis pathway
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced to formate then formaldehyde via hydrothermal chemistry, formaldehyde reacts with HCN to form glycolonitrile via cyanosulfidic chemistry on stibnite surfaces, glycolonitrile undergoes ammonolysis to aminoacetonitrile on montmorillonite, and aminoacetonitrile is hydrolyzed to glycine.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch–cyanosulfidic–ammonolysis pathway
pathways.description: CO₂ → CO → formaldehyde via FTT on magnetite, then formaldehyde → glycolonitrile → aminoacetonitrile → glycine through cyanosulfidic and ammonolysis chemistry.

pathways.name: Formate–formose–glyoxylate reductive amination pathway
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced to formate then formaldehyde hydrothermally, formaldehyde undergoes formose condensation to glycolaldehyde on borate minerals, glycolaldehyde is photooxidized to glycolic acid on TiO₂, glycolic acid is further photooxidized to glyoxylate, and glyoxylate undergoes reductive amination with NH₃ and Fe⁰ to produce glycine. This pathway avoids HCN entirely.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch–formose–glyoxylate reductive amination pathway
pathways.description: CO₂ → CO → formaldehyde via FTT on magnetite, then formaldehyde → glycolaldehyde → glycolic acid → glyoxylate → glycine via formose, photooxidation, and reductive amination. An HCN-independent route.

pathways.name: Formate–cyanosulfidic–nitrile hydrolysis–glyoxylate pathway
pathways.description: CO₂ → formate → formaldehyde hydrothermally, then formaldehyde + HCN → glycolonitrile on stibnite, glycolonitrile is hydrolyzed to glycolic acid (releasing NH₃) on montmorillonite, glycolic acid is photooxidized to glyoxylate on TiO₂, and glyoxylate is reductively aminated to glycine.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch–cyanosulfidic–nitrile hydrolysis–glyoxylate pathway
pathways.description: CO₂ → CO → formaldehyde via FTT, then formaldehyde → glycolonitrile → glycolic acid → glyoxylate → glycine through cyanosulfidic formation, hydrolysis, photooxidation, and reductive amination.

pathways.name: Formate–mechanistic Strecker pathway (via aminomethanol)
pathways.description: CO₂ → formate → formaldehyde hydrothermally, formaldehyde condenses with NH₃ to form aminomethanol (the experimentally identified Strecker intermediate) on montmorillonite, aminomethanol reacts with HCN to form aminoacetonitrile, and aminoacetonitrile is hydrolyzed to glycine. This decomposes the Strecker synthesis into its individual mechanistic steps.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch–mechanistic Strecker pathway (via aminomethanol)
pathways.description: CO₂ → CO → formaldehyde via FTT on magnetite, formaldehyde → aminomethanol → aminoacetonitrile → glycine via the stepwise mechanistic Strecker synthesis. Provides the FTT-based alternative entry into the aminomethanol route.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Short, well-supported pathway. Strecker synthesis is the most documented prebiotic route to glycine. rxn_004 (formate reduction to formaldehyde on native iron) is the weakest link—less experimentally validated than greigite-catalyzed formate production but thermodynamically plausible. Literature strongly supports aminoacetonitrile as key intermediate and its hydrolysis to glycine.
suggestions: Provide more specific conditions for formate-to-formaldehyde reduction, or consider citing Varma et al. for native iron catalysis.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Sound parallel to P1 using the FTT branch. rxn_003 (CO + H2 → CH2O on magnetite) is plausible but formaldehyde is typically a minor FTT product; methanol and longer hydrocarbons are more common. RWGS on magnetite is well established. Overall the pathway is viable.
suggestions: Acknowledge that formaldehyde yield from FTT is low and may require specific temperature windows (e.g., 150–250°C).

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Cyanosulfidic glycolonitrile formation is well-supported by Patel et al. and Sutherland group work. Ammonolysis of glycolonitrile to aminoacetonitrile is plausible but less experimentally characterized than direct Strecker. Literature confirms glycolonitrile as a key intermediate in cyanosulfidic networks.
suggestions: Specify sulfide mineral (e.g., CuCN or Na2S) role in rxn_006 more explicitly, and cite experimental conditions for ammonolysis.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Parallel FTT variant of P3. Same strengths and weaknesses as P3 but with the slightly less robust FTT formaldehyde step. Still above threshold.
suggestions: Same as P2 and P3 suggestions combined.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This longer pathway introduces weaker steps. Formose condensation to glycolaldehyde is well known but notoriously unselective. UV photooxidation of glycolaldehyde to glycolic acid (rxn_009) is plausible with TiO2 but sparsely documented for this specific substrate. Oxidation of glycolic acid to glyoxylate (rxn_010) is thermodynamically uphill without strong oxidant. Reductive amination of glyoxylate (rxn_015) is documented but needs explicit reducing agent (Fe⁰, pyridoxamine analog) not specified. The 'Biochemical' label for rxn_015 is slightly questionable as it typically requires mineral cofactors.
suggestions: Specify reducing agent for rxn_015 (e.g., Fe⁰ or sulfide).
Provide literature support for sequential UV photooxidation steps.
Consider whether rxn_015 is better classified as Surface given its mineral requirements.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: FTT variant of P5. Inherits all weaknesses of P5 plus the lower-confidence FTT formaldehyde step. Below approval threshold.

Hybrid pathway combining cyanosulfidic glycolonitrile formation with hydrolysis to glycolic acid and subsequent oxidation to glyoxylate. Glycolonitrile hydrolysis to glycolic acid (rxn_011) is documented. Same weaknesses as P5 for the glyoxylate branch (rxn_010, rxn_015).
suggestions: Same as P5 for the terminal glyoxylate steps.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: FTT variant of P7. Below approval threshold due to cumulative uncertainty in FTT formaldehyde and glyoxylate terminal steps.

Mechanistic Strecker pathway via aminomethanol is explicitly supported by the literature (Source 10878 identifies aminomethanol as a key experimental intermediate). The stepwise decomposition of Strecker into condensation + HCN addition is chemically more informative than the single-step rxn_005 and fully consistent with the known mechanism.
suggestions: Minor: cite the specific experimental identification of aminomethanol intermediate.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: FTT variant of P9. Slightly lower confidence due to FTT formaldehyde step but still above threshold. The mechanistic Strecker steps are strong.
suggestions: Same as P2 for FTT step.

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an excellent prebiotic synthesis network for glycine. The network demonstrates strong topology with 15 molecules, 15 reactions, 10 pathways, and 5 convergence points. Formaldehyde serves as an outstanding hub molecule (degree ≥ 6) connecting hydrothermal CO₂ fixation to multiple surface chemistry modules. The network provides two independent terminal routes to glycine (aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis and glyoxylate reductive amination), ensuring robustness. Six of ten pathways are approved (P1, P2, P3, P4, P9, P10), all via the aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis route. The glyoxylate branch pathways (P5-P8) fall just below threshold due to underspecified UV photooxidation steps and missing reducing agent in the reductive amination. The environmental flow (Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical) is strictly maintained throughout. Literature alignment is very strong: Strecker synthesis, cyanosulfidic glycolonitrile formation, aminomethanol intermediate, formose reaction, and glyoxylate reductive amination are all well-cited in prebiotic chemistry literature. Mineral catalysts (greigite, magnetite, native iron) are specified for all hydrothermal reactions. Minor concerns: (1) rxn_004 (formate reduction to formaldehyde on native iron) could use stronger experimental citation; (2) FTT formaldehyde yield is typically low; (3) the glyoxylate branch needs explicit reducing agent specification for rxn_015; (4) surface reactions should more explicitly name mineral agents in individual descriptions. Overall, a well-designed, literature-grounded network that achieves the target through multiple validated routes.
